attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the hospital at 41 weeks gestation for induction of labour. This is her first pregnancy. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. A vaginal examination is performed to assess the Bishop score prior to management of prolonged pregnancy.

The examination reveals:

  • Cervical dilation: 1 cm
  • Cervical effacement: 30%
  • Cervical consistency: Firm
  • Cervical position: Posterior
  • Fetal station: -2

The Bishop score is calculated using the following table:

Factor0123
Dilation (cm)Closed1-23-45+
Effacement (%)0-3040-5060-7080+
ConsistencyFirmMediumSoft-
PositionPosteriorMidAnterior-
Station-3-2-1/0+1/+2
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Artificial rupture of membranes and oxytocin infusion
attributes.explanation:
Based on the examination findings, the Bishop score for this patient is 2. Let's break down the scoring:

1. Cervical dilation: 1 cm = 1 point
2. Cervical effacement: 30% = 0 points
3. Cervical consistency: Firm = 0 points
4. Cervical position: Posterior = 0 points
5. Fetal station: -2 = 1 point

The total sum is 1 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 1 = 2 points

A Bishop score of 2 indicates an unfavourable cervix for induction of labour. In such cases, cervical ripening is typically recommended before proceeding with other induction methods. Prostaglandin is the most appropriate next step in management. Prostaglandins (such as dinoprostone or misoprostol) are effective for cervical ripening and can increase the likelihood of successful induction.

Artificial rupture of membranes (ARM) is not recommended as the initial step with an unfavourable cervix, as it may lead to prolonged labour and increased risk of infection. ARM is more suitable when the cervix is more favourable (Bishop score ≥6).

Artificial rupture of membranes and oxytocin infusion combination is also not appropriate as the initial management for an unfavourable cervix. This approach is more suitable after cervical ripening has been achieved.

Oxytocin infusion alone is not the best first choice with an unfavourable cervix. Oxytocin is more effective when the cervix is more favourable and is often used after cervical ripening or in combination with ARM.

Caesarean section is not indicated at this stage. It would only be considered if there were contraindications to vaginal delivery or if induction methods fail after adequate attempts.

The Bishop score is a valuable tool in assessing cervical readiness for induction. A score of 6 or more generally indicates a favourable cervix. With a low Bishop score, as in this case, cervical ripening methods are typically employed first to improve the chances of successful induction and reduce the risk of prolonged labour or failed induction.
learningPoints: The Bishop score is used to assess cervical readiness for induction of labour, with a score of 6 or more indicating a favourable cervix.
The Bishop score includes five components: cervical dilation, effacement, consistency, position, and fetal station.
A low Bishop score may indicate the need for cervical ripening before proceeding with artificial rupture of membranes.
references: Queensland Health. Induction of Labour Clinical Guideline [Internet]. Queensland: Queensland Health; 2023. Available from: https://www.health.qld.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0020/641423/g-iol.pdf

Wormer KC, Bauer A, Williford AE. Bishop Score. [Updated 2024 Jul 17]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470368/
